amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: E. coli responds to DNA damage by coordinating repair with cell cycle progression through the SOS response and replication checkpoint mechanisms. When replication forks encounter DNA lesions, RecA nucleoprotein filaments form on single-stranded DNA, activate LexA autocleavage (inducing SOS genes for DNA repair), and inhibit cell division by blocking FtsZ ring assembly through SulA protein. RecN protein acts early in the damage response to hold broken chromosomes together for repair. The DpiAB two-component system provides an additional checkpoint sensing replication stress. This multi-layered response ensures that cells do not divide with damaged DNA, allowing time for repair before resuming growth. The checkpoint is relieved when repair is complete, RecA filaments disassemble, LexA repression is restored, and SulA is degraded by Lon protease. This flowchart illustrates damage detection, checkpoint activation, cell cycle arrest, repair coordination, and checkpoint recovery.

    Start([DNA Damage Checkpoint]) --> A1{Type of DNA damage?}

    A1 -->|Replication fork stall| A3[Replication stress checkpoint]

    A8 --> A9[/Prevents chromosome fragmentation/]
    
    A3 --> A10[Replication fork encounters lesion]
    A10 --> A11[Fork stalls - helicase uncoupled from polymerase]

    A4 --> A13[/UV lesion blocks replication/]

    A14 --> A12

    A17 --> A19[RecA monomers loaded on ssDNA]
    A18 --> A19
    
    A19 --> A20[RecA polymerizes on ssDNA 5' to 3']

    A21 --> A22[RecA filament binding ATP]
    
    A22 --> A23[RecA filament is activated coprotease]
    
    A23 --> A24[RecA stimulates LexA autocleavage]
    A24 --> A25[/LexA is transcriptional repressor of SOS genes/]
    A25 --> A26[LexA normally dimeric bound to SOS boxes]

    A32 --> A33[\SOS genes derepressed/]
    A33 --> A34{Which SOS genes induced?}
    
    A34 -->|recA| A35[RecA - positive feedback]
    A34 -->|lexA| A36[LexA - negative feedback]
    A34 -->|sulA| A37[/SulA cell division inhibitor/]
    A34 -->|uvrA uvrB| A38[UvrAB for NER]
    A34 -->|recN| A39[RecN for DSB repair]
    A34 -->|dinB umuDC| A40[Error-prone polymerases]
    
    A35 --> A41[More RecA - amplifies response]
    A36 --> A42[More LexA - prepares for recovery]
    
    A37 --> A43[SulA protein synthesized]
    A43 --> A44[/SulA is FtsZ inhibitor/]
    A44 --> A45[SulA binds FtsZ monomers]
    A45 --> A46[\FtsZ polymerization blocked/]
    A46 --> A47[/Z-ring cannot form/]
    A47 --> A48[Cell division arrested]
    
    A48 --> A49[Checkpoint activated - cell elongates]
    A49 --> A50[/Filamentous cell phenotype/]
    
    A38 --> A51[DNA repair enzymes active]
    A39 --> A51
    A40 --> A52[Translesion synthesis - mutagenic but allows survival]
    
    A51 --> A53{Repair successful?}
    A52 --> A53
    
    A53 -->|Yes| A54[DNA damage repaired]
    A53 -->|No - persistent| A55[Continued SOS induction]
    
    A54 --> A56[ssDNA eliminated]
    A56 --> A57[RecA filaments disassemble]
    A57 --> A58[ATP hydrolysis and RecA recycling]
    
    A58 --> A59[RecA coprotease activity lost]
    A59 --> A60[/LexA cleavage stops/]
    A60 --> A61[New LexA accumulates]
    A61 --> A62[LexA binds SOS boxes]
    A62 --> A63[\SOS genes repressed/]
    
    A63 --> A64[/sulA transcription stops/]
    A64 --> A65[/Existing SulA degraded by Lon protease/]
    A65 --> A66[SulA half-life ~2 min]
    A66 --> A67[SulA levels drop]
    
    A67 --> A68[/FtsZ released from inhibition/]
    A68 --> A69[FtsZ polymerization resumes]
    A69 --> A70[Z-ring forms at midcell]
    A70 --> A71[Cell division proceeds]
    
    A71 --> A72[Checkpoint released]
    A72 --> A73[Normal cell cycle resumes]
    
    A9 --> A74[RecN maintains chromosome organization during repair]
    A74 --> A51
    
    A55 --> A75[Chronic SOS state]
    A75 --> A76{DpiAB checkpoint also active}
    A76 -->|Yes| A77[DpiB sensor kinase activated by replication stress]
    A77 --> A78[DpiA~P response regulator]
    A78 --> A79[Additional checkpoint genes induced]
    
    A79 --> A80[Coordinate DNA repair and cell cycle]
    A51 --> A80
    
    A80 --> A81{Ultimate outcome?}
    A81 -->|Repair successful| A54
    A81 -->|Repair fails| A82[Cell death or mutation]
    
    A28 --> End1([Normal growth - no checkpoint])
    A73 --> End2([Checkpoint recovery - resume growth])
    A82 --> End3([Cell death or mutagenesis])

name: DNA Damage Checkpoint and Cell Cycle Arrest
notes: The DNA damage checkpoint illustrates coordination between DNA repair and cell division. SulA-mediated FtsZ inhibition ensures cells don't divide with damaged DNA (preventing 'guillotining' of chromosomes). RecN acts early to hold broken chromosomes together. The checkpoint has elegant feedback: RecA induces its own expression (amplification) and LexA expression (preparation for recovery). SulA's rapid degradation by Lon protease once damage is repaired allows quick checkpoint release. In eukaryotes, similar checkpoint principles exist (p53, CHK1/CHK2) but with more complex regulation. Dysregulation of checkpoints leads to genomic instability and cancer in eukaryotes, or cell death/mutagenesis in bacteria.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on extensive genetic and biochemical studies of E. coli DNA damage responses. All components (RecA, LexA, SulA, RecN, DpiAB), mechanisms (RecA activation, LexA cleavage, FtsZ inhibition), and checkpoint logic have been well-characterized. The model represents how bacteria coordinate DNA repair with cell division.
